ai_draft_to_edit:
The paper proposes a video prediction model that predicts local transformations rather than future pixels directly. The method first estimates affine transformations between adjacent frames on a dense grid of overlapping patches, then trains a convolutional network to predict future affine transform fields from past transform fields. At test time, predicted transforms are applied to the most recent frame to synthesize future frames. The paper argues that this avoids the blurriness typical of pixel-space regression because uncertainty is handled in transformation space, where averaging errors may still correspond to plausible motion. A second contribution is an evaluation protocol in which generated frames are passed to an action classifier trained on real videos, with classification accuracy used as a proxy for whether the generated sequence preserves discriminative content. Experiments on moving MNIST and UCF-101 suggest that the proposed method produces sharper outputs than pixel-regression or adversarial baselines and obtains higher downstream classification accuracy while using substantially fewer parameters and computation.

The main strength of the paper is the clarity and simplicity of the central idea. Modeling local motion explicitly is a natural and appealing alternative to direct pixel prediction, and the paper presents a concrete instantiation that is easy to understand. The decomposition into affine transform extraction, transform prediction, and frame reconstruction makes the approach modular. The use of overlapping patches is also sensible: it provides more flexibility than a single global transform while retaining a structured, low-dimensional prediction target. The unrolled multi-step training procedure is another positive aspect, since it directly addresses the train-test mismatch that arises when a one-step predictor is recursively applied at test time.

The paper is also valuable in emphasizing that pixel MSE is a poor measure for video prediction quality. This point is not new in spirit, but the proposed classifier-based evaluation is a practical attempt to define a task-oriented alternative. The reported difference between ground-truth frames and reconstructions from ground-truth affine transforms is particularly useful: it suggests that, at least for the tested frame sizes and patch parameters, the affine patch representation preserves much of the information needed by the action classifier. The comparison to a copy-last-frame baseline, optical flow extrapolation, and a stronger learned video prediction model is also helpful. The computational efficiency of the method is another significant advantage. If the reported parameter and floating-point operation counts are accurate and comparable, the approach is an attractive baseline for future work.

However, I have several concerns that make the paper less convincing than it could be. The largest issue is that the proposed evaluation protocol, while interesting, is not sufficient on its own to establish the quality of video prediction. Action classification accuracy on generated frames may reflect preservation of static appearance more than realistic temporal dynamics. This concern is supported by the high score of the copy-last-frame baseline, especially when using only four generated frames. Many UCF-101 classes can be recognized from scene context, objects, clothing, or pose, and the classifier may not be very sensitive to subtle motion artifacts. Thus, a model that simply keeps frames sharp and preserves the actor and background may score well even if it substantially underestimates or distorts motion. The paper acknowledges some underestimation of movement, but the proposed metric may not penalize this sufficiently.

Relatedly, the classifier-based metric has a potentially ambiguous interpretation. If the goal is to evaluate whether generated videos are plausible continuations, then retaining discriminative features is only one requirement. A generated sequence could preserve the class label while being dynamically implausible, temporally inconsistent, or nearly static. Conversely, a plausible future may change the appearance in a way that makes the classifier less confident, particularly for actions requiring longer temporal context. It would strengthen the paper to include additional quantitative measures targeted at temporal realism, such as flow statistics, human preference studies, or comparisons under classifiers trained to distinguish real from generated sequences. Even a careful breakdown by action category would help determine whether the gains arise from motion-sensitive classes or mostly from appearance-dominated ones.

A second concern is the limited experimental depth. The moving MNIST results are purely qualitative, and the UCF-101 experiments use a single main downstream classifier. There is no statistical analysis or indication of variance across splits, generated samples, or training runs. It is also unclear how sensitive the results are to the affine extraction procedure, which appears to be a crucial part of the system. The ablations on patch size, input frames, and prediction horizon are useful, but they are relatively shallow. For example, the paper reports classification accuracy changes but not reconstruction quality of the affine extraction step, transform prediction error, or the relationship between transform-space MSE and visual quality. Since the method depends heavily on the assumption that local affine transforms are a good representation, more detailed quantitative analysis of that assumption would be valuable.

The affine transform extractor itself needs more explanation. The paper describes estimating a set of affine mappings by minimizing reconstruction error, but it does not provide enough detail about the optimization procedure, initialization, convergence criteria, boundary handling, regularization, or computational cost. Are the affine transforms estimated independently for every pair of frames in the training set through iterative optimization? If so, how expensive is this preprocessing stage compared with training a direct prediction model? Are there failure cases when illumination changes, occlusions, or non-rigid deformations cause poor local affine estimates? Since the learned predictor is trained on these estimated transforms, noise or ambiguity in the extraction stage could substantially affect performance. The paper would benefit from a more complete description and analysis of this stage.

The method also has inherent representational limitations that deserve more serious discussion. Applying local transforms to the previous frame cannot synthesize newly visible content, model disocclusions, handle significant out-of-plane rotations, or represent appearance changes except through warping. Averaging overlapping transformed patches may hide some artifacts, but it cannot solve the missing-content problem. On short horizons this may be acceptable, but for longer prediction it should cause degradation. The paper reports results up to eight frames, but the conclusions sometimes sound broader than the evidence supports. It would be useful to see how quickly the method fails as the prediction horizon increases, and whether failures are qualitatively different from those of pixel-space models.

The comparison to prior learned prediction models is promising but not fully satisfying. The paper compares to a multi-scale adversarial model and notes that it is much larger and more expensive. However, it is not entirely clear whether all models were trained under comparable conditions, with the same input preprocessing, frame resolution, number of conditioning frames, and prediction horizon. Since the proposed method predicts transformations extracted from frames, while the baseline predicts pixels, the training targets and losses differ substantially. This is the point of the paper, but it also makes fairness important. More details about baseline implementation, hyperparameter tuning, and whether the reported results are from retraining or from existing models would improve confidence.

I also find the claim that MSE in transformation space avoids averaging somewhat overstated. The paper itself observes that the model underestimates movement, which is exactly an averaging effect. Predicting the mean of plausible transformations may produce a sharp frame because the previous image is warped rather than regressed, but it can still yield an implausible or overly conservative trajectory. This is a meaningful distinction, but the paper should be more precise: the approach avoids pixel blurring due to averaging in image space, not uncertainty averaging more generally. If multiple futures are possible, a deterministic MSE-trained transform predictor still has the usual conditional mean problem. Modeling a distribution over transformations, as mentioned in the conclusion, seems important rather than merely a future extension.

I have several specific questions for the authors. How exactly are the affine transforms estimated in practice, and how long does this preprocessing take on UCF-101? Are the reported computational savings measured only for the prediction network, or do they include affine extraction for conditioning frames at test time? How sensitive is the method to the stride and overlap used in patch reconstruction? Does the classifier-based evaluation use only generated frames, or are the conditioning frames also included as input to the classifier? If conditioning frames are excluded, how are short clips aligned with the classifier’s expected temporal input? If they are included, how much of the classification accuracy comes from the real conditioning frames rather than the generated continuation? Can the authors report category-level accuracies, especially for actions where temporal motion is essential? Finally, can the authors provide quantitative results for longer horizons, even if performance degrades, to clarify the useful operating range of the method?

Overall, I find the paper interesting and potentially useful, particularly as a strong and efficient baseline for short-term video prediction. The central idea of predicting local transformations is intuitive, well motivated, and produces sharper outputs than direct pixel regression. The proposed evaluation protocol is also a worthwhile contribution in that it moves away from pixel MSE. At the same time, the empirical case is not yet fully convincing. The evaluation may reward preservation of appearance more than realistic motion, the affine extraction stage is under-specified, and the method’s limitations for occlusion, disocclusion, and long-term prediction are not sufficiently quantified. My recommendation is therefore borderline, leaning weak reject in its current form. With a more rigorous evaluation of temporal realism, clearer implementation details, and stronger baseline comparisons, this could become a compelling contribution.

The paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework for solving combinatorial optimization problems with neural networks, focusing primarily on the Euclidean traveling salesman problem and briefly demonstrating applicability to Knapsack. The central idea is to use a pointer-network-style recurrent model as a stochastic policy over permutations, train it with policy gradients using tour length as the reward signal, and then improve inference by either sampling many candidate tours or performing “active search,” in which the policy parameters are further optimized on a single test instance. The paper argues that this avoids the need for supervised optimal labels, can learn useful heuristics from reward alone, and provides a relatively general recipe for neural approaches to combinatorial search. Empirically, the method substantially improves over a supervised pointer network baseline and obtains near-optimal results on random 2D Euclidean TSP instances up to 100 nodes when allowed extensive sampling or test-time optimization.

This is an interesting and timely paper. Its main contribution is not that it competes with specialized TSP solvers, which it explicitly does not, but that it demonstrates a plausible way to train neural combinatorial policies directly against an optimization objective rather than by imitation of an external solver. This distinction is important. Supervised learning from solver outputs can be brittle, expensive to scale to new problems, and misaligned with the ultimate objective, since reproducing a particular tour is not the same as finding any short tour. Framing the problem as reinforcement learning is natural, and the paper’s results show that this choice matters: the RL-trained policies outperform the supervised pointer-network approach by a large margin. The active search component is also conceptually appealing, since combinatorial optimization at test time is inherently a search problem, and the paper shows that gradient-based adaptation of the neural policy can be used as a form of instance-specific search.

A second strength is that the empirical presentation is relatively transparent about the limitations of the method. The authors compare against exact and heuristic TSP solvers and clearly state that their method is much slower than Concorde or strong local search methods. This honesty makes the paper more credible. The comparisons to Christofides-style heuristics, generic OR tools, and optimal solutions provide useful context for assessing where the learned method stands. The percentile plots of tour length ratios are particularly helpful, since average tour length alone can hide whether a method is consistently near-optimal or only occasionally succeeds. The paper also examines several inference configurations, including greedy decoding, ensembling, sampling, active search from a pretrained policy, and active search from scratch. This helps disentangle the contribution of learned initialization from the contribution of sheer test-time computation.

The paper is also significant because it opens a broader research direction. The proposed architecture and training recipe suggest that neural networks can learn reusable construction heuristics for structured discrete problems, and the use of a pointer mechanism is a reasonable architectural choice for problems whose solutions are permutations or subsets of inputs. Even though the Knapsack experiment appears less developed than the TSP section, it supports the claim that the approach is not entirely specific to tours. The discussion of feasibility constraints and penalties is useful, and it shows that the authors are aware of the challenges in extending the method to more constrained combinatorial settings.

That said, the paper has several important weaknesses. The most serious is that the empirical evidence for general combinatorial optimization is still narrow. The main experiments are on random Euclidean TSP instances with points sampled uniformly in the unit square. This distribution is standard and convenient, but it is also much simpler than many real routing problems and may strongly favor learning geometric regularities. It is unclear how well the learned policy transfers to non-uniform point distributions, clustered instances, non-Euclidean metrics, asymmetric TSP, or instances with additional constraints. Since the paper’s motivation emphasizes generality and reduced heuristic engineering, the evaluation should stress-test distribution shift more directly. A policy trained on uniform random TSP instances might be learning a highly distribution-specific geometric prior rather than a broadly useful optimization strategy.

The second major weakness is computational efficiency. The best results rely on sampling or active search over very large numbers of candidate solutions, with reported runtimes that are orders of magnitude worse than specialized solvers and often worse than reasonably generic local search. This does not invalidate the research contribution, but it weakens some of the practical framing. In particular, if a method requires 1.28 million sampled tours or many thousands of gradient updates per test instance, it is difficult to view it as an effective solver rather than as an interesting proof of concept. The paper would benefit from a more careful analysis of the tradeoff between computation and solution quality, especially relative to simple baselines that also scale with test-time budget, such as randomized 2-opt, repeated nearest-neighbor starts, or local improvement applied to neural samples. The comparison to OR tools is useful, but the neural method’s advantage over some generic baselines may disappear under equal wall-clock or equal candidate-evaluation budgets.

The active search method is intriguing but not fully understood. Optimizing the policy on a single instance from sampled rewards resembles a learned stochastic local search procedure, but the paper does not provide enough analysis of why it works, when it fails, or what role the pretrained policy plays beyond initializing a distribution biased toward good tours. For TSP50, sampling appears competitive with or better than active search, while for TSP100 active search helps more. This difference deserves explanation. Does active search mainly improve because it reduces entropy around good regions, because it learns instance-specific geometric structure, or because it acts like an adaptive sampling temperature? How sensitive is it to learning rate, baseline decay, batch size, number of glimpses, and input shuffling? Since active search is one of the paper’s distinctive ideas, stronger ablation and diagnostic experiments would make the contribution much clearer.

The supervised baseline also raises concerns. The authors state that their supervised implementation trained on optimal tours performs worse than the results reported in prior work and that learning from optimal tours may be harder due to subtle features. This is plausible, but not entirely satisfying. Since one of the main empirical claims is that RL substantially improves over supervised learning, the supervised baseline needs to be as strong and carefully tuned as possible. It would be useful to compare supervised pretraining followed by RL fine-tuning, imitation of multiple optimal or near-optimal tours when symmetries exist, and supervised training with the same search procedures used for the RL policy. Without these controls, it is hard to know whether the improvement comes from reinforcement learning per se, from differences in training setup, or from the use of test-time sampling and optimization.

The paper’s claims about generality should also be moderated. The pointer network architecture is well matched to selecting elements of an input sequence, especially when feasibility can be enforced by masking previously selected items. However, many combinatorial optimization problems have richer state-dependent constraints, graph structures, or solution representations that are not naturally captured by a sequence-to-sequence model over input indices. The discussion of penalties for infeasible solutions is reasonable but speculative; for problems where feasible solutions are rare, naive penalty methods may produce extremely sparse or uninformative rewards. The Knapsack result helps, but Knapsack is still a relatively simple setting compared with vehicle routing with time windows, graph coloring, scheduling, or integer programming. Stronger evidence on a constrained problem where feasibility is genuinely difficult would better support the broad framing.

I would like the authors to clarify several points. First, how robust are the trained TSP policies to changes in the test distribution, such as clustered cities, points sampled from non-square domains, mixtures of distributions, or larger graph sizes than those used in training? Second, how much of the improvement from active search depends on random shuffling of the input sequence, and does the model’s performance vary significantly with different input orderings at greedy decoding time? Third, when using 1.28 million samples, how many distinct tours are actually produced, and how concentrated is the policy distribution before and after active search? Fourth, can the authors provide an equal-time comparison against simple randomized local-search baselines, including local improvement initialized from the model’s own tours? Such a hybrid baseline would be particularly informative, because it might reveal whether the neural network is most useful as a generator of good initial solutions rather than as a complete solver. Fifth, for Knapsack, what are the instance distributions, capacities, item value/weight distributions, and comparisons to dynamic programming or greedy baselines? The claim of optimality up to 200 items is encouraging but difficult to interpret without more details.

I would also encourage the authors to expand the ablation study. The critic architecture, number of glimpses, logit clipping, temperature tuning, model size, and batch size all appear potentially important. Since policy-gradient methods can be unstable and sensitive to variance reduction, it would be helpful to know which components are essential. For example, how much worse is a moving-average baseline than the learned critic during pretraining? Does increasing the number of glimpses improve TSP100 but hurt speed? Does the model learn meaningful geometric behavior, such as avoiding crossings, and if so at what stage of training does this emerge? Visualizing failure cases could also improve the paper, particularly for greedy decoding versus sampling and active search.

Overall, my recommendation is weak accept. The method is not competitive with mature combinatorial optimization solvers in speed, scale, or final solution quality, and the empirical evaluation is limited mainly to uniform random Euclidean TSP. Nevertheless, the paper presents a clear and promising formulation of neural combinatorial optimization as reinforcement learning, demonstrates a substantial improvement over supervised pointer-network training, and introduces test-time active search as an interesting bridge between learned policies and instance-specific optimization. The work is likely to stimulate further research on learned heuristics, neural construction policies, and hybrid neural-search methods. I would be more enthusiastic if the paper included stronger distribution-shift experiments, more rigorous equal-compute baselines, and a deeper analysis of active search, but even in its current form it makes a worthwhile conceptual and empirical contribution.

The paper proposes compositional kernel machines, an instance-based visual classification method intended to combine some advantages of kernel methods, nearest-neighbor methods, and convolutional architectures. The central idea is to compare a query image not only to observed training images, but to an exponentially large implicit set of “virtual instances” formed by recombining local elements extracted from training examples. A sum-product-function representation is used to make inference over this exponentially large set tractable under decomposability constraints. The authors define CKMs recursively in terms of leaf kernels, products over disjoint scopes, and weighted sums over common scopes, and they describe both a uniform-weight version and a discriminatively trained version using leave-one-out squared-hinge loss. Experiments on small NORB and two derived NORB variants suggest that the approach can improve over standard instance-based methods and can be competitive with some neural baselines in low-data compositional and symmetry-heavy settings.

The paper’s main strength is that it explores an interesting middle ground between classical nonparametric/kernel methods and deep compositional models. The motivation is compelling: if visual categories are compositional and exhibit local symmetries, then a method that can recombine observed parts should require fewer full-image examples than a purely global nearest-neighbor or SVM approach. The use of sum-product structure to compactly represent many possible recombinations is natural and potentially powerful. I also appreciated the attempt to make the connection to sample complexity concrete: rather than merely arguing that compositions should help, the authors implement a model that can actually sum or maximize over many latent part assignments and test it on settings designed to isolate composition and symmetry.

Another positive aspect is the paper’s conceptual clarity at the high level. The definition of CKMs as recursively composed sums and products is simple, and the analogy to sum-product networks and image grammars is useful. The claim that SVMs and nearest-neighbor-like methods can be viewed as special cases helps position the method. The leave-one-out training objective is also sensible for an instance-based learner, and the distinction between CKM with uniform weights and CKMW with learned weights is helpful experimentally. The paper is ambitious in trying to provide a new family of models rather than a single ad hoc architecture.

The experimental section contains some encouraging evidence. The improvement over raw nearest-neighbor methods on small NORB supports the claim that local composition can mitigate the curse of dimensionality relative to global image matching. The synthetic NORB Compositions experiment is a reasonable attempt to test whether a method can generalize from observed parts to unseen combinations. In that setting, the reported advantage over a conventionally trained convolutional network is plausible and interesting, especially given the small number of training examples. The NORB Symmetries learning curves also tell a coherent story: the proposed method appears to benefit from additional examples without requiring extensive retraining, and the learned-weight variant starts to improve as more data become available.

That said, I have several significant concerns that prevent me from being convinced by the paper in its current form. The first is that the term “kernel machine” is used somewhat loosely. The theoretical presentation begins from kernel methods and invokes kernel composition, but the actual experimental model uses a max-sum semiring and arbitrary local scoring functions, including costs and thresholds. It is not clear that the resulting discriminant corresponds to a positive semidefinite kernel, or even that the method should be analyzed as a kernel method rather than as a latent structured matching model. This matters because some of the paper’s motivation relies on the training advantages of kernel machines, but the optimization objective is later acknowledged to be generally nonconvex. If CKMs are primarily max-product latent-composition models, then the relationship to SVMs is more limited than the framing suggests.

A related issue is that the theoretical contribution seems underdeveloped. The sum-product theorem establishes that a decomposable sum-product function can be evaluated in time linear in its size, but this is a property inherited from the chosen representation rather than a new algorithmic result specific to CKMs. The important question is how large the representation must be to capture useful visual compositions. The experimental architecture includes unary and pairwise max nodes over ORB features, but equation-level descriptions suggest potentially very large pairwise maximizations over all training elements. The paper mentions approximate nearest-neighbor structures and pruning, yet the exact computational complexity of the implemented model is not sufficiently transparent. The claim of summing over exponentially many virtual instances is formally true for decomposable structures, but it risks overstating the practical modeling power if the architecture only captures limited local pairwise structure.

The experimental evidence is also too narrow and, in places, not fully fair to the baselines. All experiments are based on NORB or constructed variants of NORB. NORB is useful for controlled studies, but it is small and stylized, and the paper makes broad claims about object recognition and alternatives to convolutional networks. The convolutional baselines are not described in enough detail to assess their strength. For example, on the NORB Compositions task the convnet achieves near-chance accuracy, but this may reflect an unsuitable architecture, insufficient augmentation, or a poor training protocol rather than a fundamental limitation. Similarly, comparison to an SVM using features from a pretrained Inception model is awkward because those features come from a very different image distribution and may be poorly matched to grayscale toy images. The small NORB comparison uses numbers from prior work in some cases and the authors’ own implementation in others, which makes it difficult to disentangle algorithmic improvements from differences in preprocessing, feature extraction, and hyperparameter tuning.

The synthetic datasets raise further questions. The NORB Compositions task is interesting, but it is constructed in a way that may strongly favor local feature matching. If the label is determined by the identity of local toy instances appearing in an image, and if ORB descriptors can match those instances directly, then the task may be closer to multi-object instance retrieval than to semantic compositional generalization. That is still a valid problem, but it is narrower than the discussion suggests. It would be useful to know how the method performs when the relevant parts vary more substantially, when distractors are present, or when local descriptors are less reliable. The NORB Symmetries experiment similarly seems well suited to an ORB-based method because ORB descriptors already provide some rotation invariance. The paper should isolate how much of the gain comes from the CKM composition mechanism versus the hand-engineered invariances in the local features.

I also found the learning procedure insufficiently specified and evaluated. The leave-one-out objective is appealing, but it is not clear how expensive exact training is as the number of images and elements grows, especially with pairwise terms. The authors state that gradients can be computed efficiently and that small leaf-kernel values may be excluded, but the distinction between exact and approximate training is not clear in the experiments. There is little discussion of regularization, constraints on weights, initialization beyond uniform weights, or sensitivity to hyperparameters. Since the method includes ORB extraction parameters, leaf-kernel parameters, cost-function parameters, approximate search parameters, and architectural parameters, a more systematic analysis is needed. The paper criticizes convolutional networks for difficult hyperparameter tuning, but CKMs appear to introduce a substantial design space of their own.

Another concern is representational scalability. The paper argues that CKMs have advantages when new data arrive because training can be fast and instance-based. However, the model size grows with the training set, and test-time computation appears substantially slower than the reported convolutional network testing time. This tradeoff may be acceptable in some regimes, but the paper should be more precise about memory requirements, indexing costs, update costs, and test-time latency as datasets grow beyond NORB scale. In particular, the claim that adding instances and categories is easy should be supported by incremental experiments, not just asserted. For real object recognition tasks, storing and searching over all local features from all training images could become a serious bottleneck.

I would like the authors to clarify several points. In what precise sense is the experimental CKM a kernel machine? Does the max-sum version define a positive semidefinite kernel or is it better viewed as a latent matching discriminant? How much of the empirical improvement comes from ORB features alone, from local position-aware matching, from the pairwise source-image coherence cost, and from the sum-product architecture? Can the authors include ablations removing each of these components? How are hyperparameters selected, and are validation images disjoint in object identity and transformation from the test images? For the pairwise max nodes, is the maximization over all pairs of training elements actually performed, approximated by nearest-neighbor search, or pruned by thresholds? What are the memory and runtime costs as a function of the number of training images and extracted elements? Finally, can the method be evaluated on at least one less controlled dataset, or on a stronger modern convnet baseline trained with a protocol appropriate for small data?

Overall, I find the paper creative and potentially promising, but not yet convincing as a strong ICLR contribution. The core idea of implicit compositional virtual instances is interesting, and the connection to tractable sum-product evaluation could lead to useful models. However, the current submission overstates the kernel-method interpretation, relies on limited and somewhat favorable experiments, and does not provide enough ablation or scalability analysis to establish the practical value of the approach. My recommendation is weak reject for the main conference. I would be enthusiastic about seeing a revised version with clearer theoretical positioning, stronger baselines, more detailed computational analysis, and broader empirical validation.

The paper addresses the problem of producing answers to long, non-factoid questions, with a focus on love-advice questions from a Japanese community QA service. The proposed system has two main components. First, it learns word embeddings that are biased by document-level semantic information such as question categories and title terms, with the goal of making words like “relationship” acquire different relevance in different topical contexts. Second, rather than selecting a whole existing answer, it constructs an answer by selecting and combining shorter sentence units of predefined types, such as conclusion and supplement. The model uses bidirectional LSTMs to represent questions and candidate answer sentences, a hinge-loss objective over correct and incorrect sentence combinations, and an attention mechanism from a selected conclusion sentence to a supplemental sentence. Experiments on Oshiete goo data suggest improvements over LSTM-based answer selection baselines, particularly in a harder answer construction setting, and the authors report deployment in a live love-advice service.

The paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem. Non-factoid QA is indeed an important setting where answer selection from a fixed archive is often insufficient, and the paper is right to emphasize that advice questions require sensitivity to situation, tone, and discourse structure. The attempt to move from whole-answer retrieval to sentence-level composition is a reasonable intermediate step between retrieval and full neural generation. This is especially sensible for advice domains, where fully generative systems can produce fluent but unsafe, generic, or incoherent content. By constraining the output to human-authored sentence fragments and a predefined rhetorical scenario, the system may offer a more controllable way to produce answers than unconstrained sequence generation.

A second strength is the paper’s attention to domain-dependent word usage. Using category and title information as semantic context for representation learning is a simple but plausible idea, and it addresses a real weakness of generic embeddings in community QA data. The paper gives an intuitive motivation: certain words become important only in a particular question category, while others are common across many categories and should not dominate matching. The experimental comparison between the ordinary LSTM baseline and the semantic-embedding variant suggests that this component contributes to the final performance.

The answer construction formulation is also appealing in its high-level design. The authors identify that answers in a domain such as love advice often have a stereotyped discourse structure, and they use this observation to decompose answer generation into selecting sentences for specific roles. This is a useful engineering insight. The model’s joint objective, which considers both question-sentence relevance and sentence-sentence compatibility, is preferable to independently selecting the best conclusion and best supplement. The inclusion of an attention mechanism from the conclusion to the supplement is also conceptually appropriate, since the supplemental sentence should elaborate on the same topic or rationale as the conclusion.

The practical orientation of the work is another positive aspect. The paper is based on a real QA service, uses a non-English dataset, and reports not only offline metrics but also some human judgments and deployment statistics. Many papers on QA rely on sanitized benchmark data and do not consider how a system behaves when used by real people. The fact that the authors attempted to deploy the model and collect user feedback makes the work potentially valuable to the community.

However, the paper has several significant weaknesses that limit its suitability for publication in its current form. The most important issue is that the claimed contribution as “answer generation” is overstated. The system does not generate novel sentences; it retrieves and recombines sentence fragments extracted from existing answers according to a manually specified scenario. This is a legitimate task, but it should be described more precisely as answer construction or sentence-level answer composition. The introduction repeatedly contrasts the method with answer selection and presents it as moving beyond selection, but the system is still fundamentally extractive. The paper would be stronger if it clearly defined the task as constrained extractive answer composition and discussed its limitations relative to genuine generation.

The evaluation methodology is not sufficiently convincing. The main offline construction metric measures whether the model can recreate original conclusion-supplement pairs. This is a very narrow proxy for answer quality. A constructed answer can be useful even if it does not match the original pair, and conversely an original pair may not be the only or best answer to a question. Average precision over exact or annotated original pairs therefore may underestimate or mischaracterize the task. At the same time, because many candidate sentences may be generic advice statements, the metric may also reward superficial matching. The paper needs a clearer definition of what counts as a correct constructed answer, how many gold combinations exist per question, and whether correctness was judged semantically or simply by identity with extracted sentences.

The human evaluation is promising but underdescribed. The paper reports judgments by two experts, but it does not specify the number of questions evaluated beyond the counts in the table, how questions were sampled, whether the experts were blind to system identity, how disagreements were resolved, or what inter-annotator agreement was. The four-point rating scale is reasonable, but the evaluation seems to compare only the proposed method against one baseline, not against all relevant variants. It also does not include human-written answers as an upper bound. Without these details, it is difficult to interpret the reported improvement. The deployment statistic that 455 out of 1,492 answers were judged “Good” is interesting, but it is not a controlled evaluation and may be affected by user expectations, interface effects, or selection bias.

The baselines and ablations are also incomplete. The comparison includes LSTM answer selection and variants of the proposed method, but the paper does not compare against simpler retrieval or composition baselines that might be quite strong. For example, selecting the conclusion and supplement independently using BM25, TF-IDF, or embedding similarity; selecting the best original answer and extracting its first conclusion-like and supplement-like sentences; or using a learned pairwise ranker with handcrafted lexical features would help establish whether the neural architecture is necessary. The paper claims that the model avoids bad sentence combinations, but it would be useful to see a direct comparison to a two-stage system that first retrieves top relevant conclusions and then ranks supplements by sentence compatibility. The current ablations do not isolate the effect of the semantic embeddings, the joint loss, pretraining, and attention as cleanly as they could.

The description of the dataset raises concerns. The answer construction corpus contains about 5,000 question-conclusion-supplement examples, which is relatively small for training several LSTM models. The paper states that conclusions and supplements were extracted by human experts, while also suggesting that sentence extraction methods could be used. Since the model depends critically on these sentence-type labels, the annotation procedure is central to the task and should be described in much more detail. How were sentence types defined? Were multiple conclusions or supplements allowed per answer? How consistent were annotators? Were candidate sentence pools restricted to the love-advice category or drawn from all categories? Were near-duplicate questions or answers present across train and test folds? Since the data comes from a community QA site, duplicate or templated answers could inflate performance if not carefully controlled.

The model presentation is sometimes unclear. The semantic embedding method is described as averaging category and title vectors with context word vectors, but the exact training objective, window size, negative sampling or softmax details, and inference-time use of category/title information are not fully specified. In the answer construction model, the notation for the loss and representations is hard to follow. The margin parameter k is stated to satisfy 0 < k < 1, but the experimental section sets k to 1.0. The loss includes several terms comparing combinations with one or both incorrect components, but it is not clear how negative conclusions and supplements are sampled, whether hard negatives are used, or how sensitive the results are to this choice. The attention mechanism also appears to compute unnormalized weights independently for each supplement token; the authors should clarify whether these weights are normalized across time, and if not, why this formulation is preferable.

The paper also lacks discussion of safety and appropriateness. A system that gives love advice to real users can influence emotionally vulnerable people. Even if the domain seems casual, advice may involve harassment, abuse, self-harm, coercion, age differences, or other sensitive situations. The proposed method recombines human-authored sentences, which can still produce inappropriate or harmful answers when taken out of context. The paper should discuss filtering, moderation, fallback behavior, and whether the predefined scenario is sufficient to avoid unsafe recommendations. This is especially important because the authors emphasize practical deployment.

I would like the authors to clarify several points. What exactly is the candidate pool at test time: all extracted conclusion and supplement sentences from the training set, from the entire QA archive, or from a held-out candidate set? Are sentences from the same original answer as the test question removed to avoid leakage? How many candidate conclusions and supplements are ranked per question, and how is computational tractability handled for all possible pairs? How are title tokens used for a new user question whose title may be absent or noisy? Does the semantic embedding method require the correct category at test time, and what happens if the category is wrong? In the human evaluation, were judges blind, how many questions were used, and what was inter-annotator agreement? Finally, can the authors provide examples of failure cases, especially cases where the conclusion is plausible but the supplement changes the intended meaning or produces socially inappropriate advice?

Overall, I view the paper as addressing an important and underexplored problem, with a practical and intuitively sensible approach. The idea of constructing non-factoid answers from typed sentence units is worth investigating, and the domain-specific embedding component is a reasonable addition. However, the current paper overclaims the generative nature of the system, does not sufficiently specify the dataset and annotation process, and relies on an evaluation that is not strong enough to establish answer quality. The experimental gains are encouraging, but the lack of strong non-neural and two-stage baselines, limited human evaluation details, and unclear risk controls make the contribution less convincing. My recommendation is weak reject. I would be more positive about a revised version that reframes the task more accurately, provides a rigorous human evaluation with blind judging and agreement statistics, includes stronger retrieval/composition baselines and ablations, and gives a fuller account of the data construction and deployment safeguards.

The paper proposes an iterative refinement approach for machine translation in which an existing complete translation is repeatedly edited by substituting individual words. The motivation is that standard left-to-right or bottom-up decoders commit to earlier decisions and cannot directly revisit them, whereas a post-hoc refinement model can condition on the entire current hypothesis and the source sentence. The authors first study a word-level error detection model, then introduce a convolutional attention-based model that predicts a replacement word at each target position given the source and surrounding target context, and finally extend this to a dual-attention model that additionally attends to the initial guess translation. At test time, the model proposes substitutions for all positions, a heuristic or classifier scores the proposed edits, and the highest-scoring edit is applied until a threshold or maximum number of edits is reached. On WMT15 German-English, starting from a phrase-based MT system, the best method improves BLEU by about 0.3–0.4 across several test sets while changing fewer than one word per sentence on average.

The main appeal of the paper is the clear formulation of translation refinement as a distinct decoding paradigm. Rather than treating translation as a strictly generative left-to-right process, the authors explore a model that has access to a full candidate output and can revise local decisions. This is a natural and potentially important direction. It addresses a real limitation of conventional decoding: many translation errors become apparent only after later context is generated, and a model that can see both left and right context may be better suited to correcting agreement, lexical choice, punctuation, or local semantic errors. The paper is also admirably concrete. The proposed models are simple enough to understand, the refinement procedure is explicit, and the experiments include several useful variants, such as single-source attention, dual attention, alternative scoring heuristics, and oracle analyses.

Another strength is that the paper does not rely on manually post-edited data. The models are trained from parallel data and system-generated hypotheses, which makes the approach more scalable than conventional automatic post-editing setups requiring human corrections of machine output. The training procedure for generating initial guesses on the training set is also thoughtfully designed: using multiple phrase-based systems trained on disjoint subsets helps avoid the unrealistic situation in which the baseline system translates sentences it has already seen during training. This is an important experimental detail and strengthens the credibility of the reported improvements.

The dual-attention model is a sensible architectural extension. Conditioning not only on the source and reference-side context during training, but also on the system’s current guess, reduces the discrepancy between training and testing and gives the model a mechanism for copying words that are already correct. The reported perplexity reduction from the single-attention model to the dual-attention model is convincing evidence that the guess translation provides useful information. The empirical finding that the dual model is more conservative also fits the task: when starting from a strong baseline, most words should be left unchanged, and a refinement model must avoid unnecessary edits. The qualitative examples further illustrate that the model can correct meaningful lexical errors, punctuation, tense, and pronoun choices.

The oracle experiments are also valuable. They show that the model’s proposed substitutions contain more potential improvement than the deployed heuristic extracts, and that the decision of when and where to edit is a major bottleneck. This analysis helps separate two issues: whether the word prediction model can suggest useful alternatives, and whether the refinement strategy can reliably choose beneficial substitutions. The gap between the heuristic method and the oracle suggests that the general idea has room for improvement, rather than merely reaching a hard limit.

However, the paper’s empirical contribution is quite modest in its current form. The best reported improvement is only around 0.4 BLEU, with mean gains of about 0.32 BLEU over the WMT test sets. While the consistency across test sets is encouraging, the magnitude is small, and the paper does not report statistical significance testing. Given the known variance of BLEU under small translation changes, it is difficult to judge whether the gains are robust enough to justify the added modeling and decoding complexity. The method modifies only about 0.6 words per sentence, which is interesting, but it also means the overall effect is limited. The paper would be much stronger if it included significance tests, bootstrap confidence intervals, or a more detailed breakdown of the types of edits that contribute to the BLEU gains.

The restriction to word substitutions is a serious limitation. Many translation errors require insertions, deletions, phrase-level replacements, reordering, or changes in segmentation. Several of the examples demonstrate exactly this problem: the model sometimes replaces a word because it cannot insert a missing function word or restructure the phrase, leading to unnatural or only partially improved output. Since the approach is motivated by human-like revision, limiting the operation set to one-for-one substitutions makes the refinement process much less expressive than the motivation suggests. The authors acknowledge this in future work, but the limitation directly affects the central claims. It is not clear whether a substitution-only model can scale beyond minor lexical cleanup.

The comparison setting is also narrow. The method is evaluated only on German-to-English and only starting from a phrase-based translation system. In 2017, neural machine translation systems are already strong baselines, and the paper’s motivation partly concerns neural left-to-right decoding. It would therefore be important to evaluate refinement on top of a neural baseline, or at least to discuss why phrase-based output is the main test case. Phrase-based systems have different error profiles from neural systems, and a model trained to fix PBMT output may mostly learn to repair local lexical and fluency errors. It remains unclear whether the approach would help strong attention-based neural systems, where errors may be more global, more fluent but semantically wrong, or related to omissions. The paper would also benefit from experiments using weaker initial guesses, stronger initial guesses, or multiple guess translations, to clarify the conditions under which iterative refinement is useful.

The novelty relative to automatic post-editing and confidence estimation is somewhat underdeveloped. The authors distinguish their approach by not requiring post-edited data, but the method still resembles monolingual or bilingual post-editing of MT output with a neural model. The paper could do more to position itself against prior work on system combination, rescoring, lattice or n-best list refinement, and post-editing trained from synthetic data. In particular, if the baseline system can produce n-best lists or lattices, a strong rescoring or minimum-risk decoding baseline might capture some of the same corrections without requiring a separate iterative editing procedure. The paper does not compare against such alternatives, making it hard to assess whether the proposed decoding scheme is the best way to use this information.

The refinement selection mechanism is another weak point. The authors test simple confidence heuristics and a small classifier, but the classifier appears to use only two log-probability features. This seems underpowered for deciding whether an edit improves translation quality. Important features such as position, source attention entropy, local language model scores, lexical identity, edit type, baseline confidence, and sentence-level context are ignored. Since the oracle analysis shows that edit selection is a major bottleneck, the paper should either present a stronger learned selection model or more clearly frame the current results as preliminary. It is also unclear how thresholds and maximum edit counts generalize across domains, language pairs, and baseline systems.

The evaluation relies almost entirely on BLEU. Because the system makes very few substitutions, BLEU may reward edits that move the output closer to the single reference even if they do not improve adequacy or fluency, and may fail to reward legitimate improvements not reflected in the reference. The error detection section itself notes the problem of single-reference evaluation, but the final evaluation does not address it. A human evaluation or targeted analysis of accepted and rejected edits would be helpful. For instance, what fraction of edits are genuinely adequacy-improving, fluency-improving, neutral paraphrases, or harmful? Are the improvements concentrated in punctuation and morphology, or do they include substantive lexical corrections? Do repeated edits ever cause oscillations or degradation? These details would make the contribution more convincing.

I have several specific questions for the authors. How sensitive are the results to the strength and type of the initial translation system? Would the same refinement model improve a neural MT baseline, or does it mainly correct artifacts of phrase-based translation? Were the BLEU improvements tested for statistical significance on each test set and on the concatenation of all test sets? How often does the model propose the identity substitution, and how often are proposed substitutions rejected by the threshold? What is the runtime cost of iterative refinement relative to baseline decoding? Does the model ever cycle between alternatives across iterations, and if so how is that handled? Why does the learned classifier not outperform the simple product heuristic, and were richer features tried? Finally, can the authors provide an edit-type analysis showing which categories of substitutions account for most of the BLEU improvement?

Overall, I find the idea promising and the paper well written, but I am not fully convinced by the empirical evidence. The work presents a clean and interesting formulation of iterative translation refinement, and the consistency of the small gains suggests that the method is doing something meaningful. At the same time, the improvements are very small, the operation set is limited, the baselines are narrow, and the evaluation does not yet establish that the method is broadly useful beyond minor corrections to phrase-based output. My recommendation is a weak reject in its current form. I would be more positive with stronger comparisons, significance testing, evaluation on neural baselines, and a more capable edit selection model or broader edit space.

The paper studies whether a convolutional network trained with weak labels on rendered tic-tac-toe boards can acquire representations corresponding to game concepts such as board locations, legal turns, and winning moves. The authors generate all legal tic-tac-toe states in which one player can win on the next move, render them as images, assign one of 18 labels corresponding to the winning side and location, train an AlexNet-style classifier with global average pooling, and visualize class activation maps. They observe that the resulting CAMs often highlight the empty square where the winning move should be placed, even though the network was not directly trained with pixel-level localization labels. Variants add grid lines or alter the piece appearance, and the paper proposes two quantitative measures intended to capture whether the activation map corresponds to the correct move. The authors interpret the results as evidence that game concepts can emerge under weak supervision, that cross-modal supervision ideas extend to higher-level action/game-rule semantics, and that CAM can activate on non-salient image regions.

The paper has an appealing central intuition. Tic-tac-toe is simple enough that the full state space can be enumerated, but still contains nontrivial relational structure: lines, turns, two players, and outcomes. Using such a controlled domain to probe what a CNN learns is a reasonable and potentially useful way to study representation learning, visualization, and weak supervision without the confounds of large natural-image datasets. The observation that activation maps can peak at empty board locations is also interesting at a qualitative level. It highlights that discriminative spatial evidence in CNNs need not coincide with object texture or local saliency, but can arise from contextual information in the receptive field. The paper is clearly motivated and tries to connect the experiment to several broader questions in deep reinforcement learning, cross-modal supervision, and interpretability. The authors also deserve credit for proposing quantitative measures rather than relying only on hand-picked visualizations, and for including appearance-change controls and an intermediate-training analysis.

However, I find the main claims substantially overstated relative to the evidence provided. The most serious issue is that the supervision is not as weak as the paper suggests. The labels are 18 classes corresponding directly to the player and square of the winning next move. Although the network is not given a segmentation mask or an explicit rule table, the class label effectively encodes the answer to the localization/action problem. In this setting, a CAM highlighting the square associated with a class is not strong evidence that the network has discovered a game concept in the sense of understanding the rule. It may simply have learned discriminative visual templates associated with each of the 18 output classes. Since the task is restricted to positions where the game ends in one move, the input-output relation can be solved by recognizing local and semi-local patterns of pieces, without representing the full rules of tic-tac-toe.

The paper’s terminology around “concepts” is therefore problematic. The claim that the network learns the concept of a grid, the winning rule, and two sides requires stronger behavioral tests. For example, if a representation encodes the winning rule, it should transfer to states where the best move blocks an opponent, creates a fork, or avoids a trap; it should generalize to board transformations or different board sizes; it should allow accurate play beyond immediate wins; or it should be usable by a simple downstream model to infer legal and winning moves under held-out configurations. The current experiment only considers a narrow subset of positions and evaluates whether the most activated region matches the already encoded class. This is closer to weakly supervised localization of a class-correlated location than to concept learning.

A second major concern is the lack of clarity about train/test separation and generalization. The paper reports 100% classification accuracy on the tic-tac-toe dataset, but it is not clear whether this is training accuracy, validation accuracy, or accuracy on a held-out set of states. Since the dataset contains only 1029 samples for the main task and is synthetically generated from a small finite state space, memorization is a real possibility. The quantitative localization scores are also not described in enough detail to assess whether they are computed on training examples or held-out examples. If all CAM visualizations and RAC/RCO scores are measured on the same examples used for fine-tuning, they provide weak evidence about learned structure. A convincing study would need held-out board states, possibly held-out board symmetries, and controls that test whether the network is learning rules rather than associating visual templates with labels.

The use of CAM as evidence for internal understanding is also not sufficiently justified. CAM is a visualization of class-discriminative spatial contributions under a particular architecture, not a direct readout of semantic concepts. A highlighted empty square may arise because the feature at that spatial location has a receptive field covering the relevant row, column, or diagonal, not because the model represents “this is the winning move” in a conceptually meaningful way. This observation is still interesting, but it should be framed more cautiously. In addition, the paper does not compare CAM to other attribution methods, occlusion tests, gradient-based saliency, or direct probing of intermediate features. Without such controls, it is difficult to know whether the heatmaps reflect robust model reasoning or artifacts of the visualization method, the rendering geometry, the receptive field layout, padding, or class-specific templates.

The experimental design would benefit from several additional baselines. A linear classifier or multilayer perceptron on symbolic 3-by-3 board encodings could establish the intrinsic difficulty of the classification task. A CNN trained from scratch, rather than fine-tuned from ImageNet AlexNet, would test whether pretraining is relevant in this synthetic domain. A model trained with randomly permuted labels or labels that encode only the side but not the square would help determine whether CAM localization is genuinely tied to the game structure. A baseline that chooses the square from simple pattern matching or from a non-convolutional classifier would contextualize the RAC values. Since the board states are tiny and discrete, the paper should also discuss whether the CNN is exploiting absolute spatial position and fixed rendering conventions rather than learning a reusable rule.

The quantitative evaluation is not yet convincing. Representation accuracy is intuitive, but the definition of “most activated patch” depends on map resolution, interpolation, patch size, and tie-breaking; these details are missing. Representation consistency is less clear: the reported values are negative and scaled by 10^3, and the interpretation of these values is not explained. If higher consistency means better alignment with an ideal activation map, the table should make that explicit and should report confidence intervals or at least variation across random initializations. The improvements from adding grid lines are plausible, but the paper attributes them to the network being given an implied board concept. An alternative explanation is simply that grid lines provide strong spatial anchors and high-frequency gradients at fixed locations, making localization by CAM easier. That is not necessarily evidence of a more semantic representation.

The connection to cross-modal supervision is also weak. In the described experiments, the labels are generated by a simulator from the same underlying board state that is rendered as an image. This is not really a separate modality in the sense used by the cited cross-modal learning papers; it is a symbolic target derived from the game engine. The paper may still be about weakly supervised representation learning from automatically generated labels, but the cross-modal framing does not add much and may confuse the contribution. Similarly, the analogy to deep reinforcement learning is only motivational. The experiments do not involve reinforcement learning, sequential decision-making, rewards, exploration, or policies. The claim that this helps answer whether a DRL network “understands” game concepts is therefore indirect at best.

I would like the authors to clarify several points. Are the reported classification and localization results computed on held-out states, or on the training set? How are train/validation/test splits constructed, and are symmetric rotations/reflections of a board allowed to appear across splits? What exactly is the label generation procedure for states with multiple winning moves, and how many such cases exist? Does the network receive only positions where the next move wins, or does it also see non-winning states during training? How is the ideal activation map defined for RCO, and why are the reported correlations negative? How sensitive are RAC and RCO to random initialization, crop/rendering details, CAM resolution, and pretraining? Finally, can the learned representation be used to select moves in states that do not end the game immediately, or to transfer to a different rendering without retraining?

Overall, I appreciate the simplicity and creativity of the experimental setup, and I think the observation about CAM activation on empty squares is worth reporting in a more focused paper on visualization behavior in synthetic relational domains. However, the current version makes much stronger claims about concept emergence, rule understanding, and cross-modal higher-level semantics than the experiments support. The task labels already encode the desired action, the evaluation appears limited to a small and possibly non-held-out dataset, and the CAM evidence is not sufficient to infer conceptual understanding. My recommendation is rejection in its present form. A revised version with rigorous held-out generalization tests, stronger baselines, clearer metrics, causal attribution experiments, and more cautious claims could become a useful case study in weakly supervised localization and probing of CNN representations.

The paper proposes a memory-augmented attention architecture for video captioning. The central idea is to improve standard attention-based sequence-to-sequence models by maintaining a recurrent memory over previous attention decisions while generating a sentence. The model has three components: a temporal encoder over video frames using convolutional feature maps and location attention, a hierarchical attention/memory module that attends over the encoded frame sequence conditioned on the decoder and memory states, and a recurrent decoder that generates the next word. The authors evaluate the method on MSVD and Charades and report competitive results, including strong BLEU scores on MSVD without using optical flow, C3D features, or fine-tuned action features.

The main strength of the submission is that it addresses a real limitation of simple video captioning models: generating a caption often requires attending not just to a single frame or local temporal region, but to a temporally extended event while keeping track of what has already been described. The proposed architecture is a plausible and intuitive way to do this. The distinction between frame-level temporal encoding, spatial/location attention within convolutional maps, and a higher-level memory over generated words and attended visual states is sensible. In particular, using convolutional feature maps rather than only fully connected CNN features gives the model the capacity to attend spatially, and the recurrent memory module can in principle reduce repeated or inconsistent attention during decoding.

A second strength is that the model is trained end-to-end and does not depend on hand-engineered semantic representations or explicit action labels. This is desirable for a captioning system, since the ultimate goal is to learn the visual-to-language mapping directly. The paper also evaluates on two datasets with different properties. MSVD is a common benchmark with many reference captions, while Charades is more challenging and action-centric, with fewer captions per video. Including Charades is useful because it tests whether the model can handle more realistic activities and less dense language supervision. The qualitative examples are also helpful in illustrating both the successes and failure modes of the method.

However, I have several significant concerns about the technical clarity, novelty, and empirical support for the claims. The first issue is that the proposed memory mechanism is not explained with sufficient precision to make its contribution fully clear. The HAM module is described as storing previous attention and an encoded version of the input videos conditioned on previously generated words, but the equations suggest a relatively standard recurrent attention mechanism: attention scores are computed from the encoded video states, the previous decoder state, and the previous memory state, then the weighted visual feature is fed into an LSTM memory. It is not obvious in what sense all previous attention distributions are explicitly stored, since the memory appears to be a compressed recurrent hidden state rather than an addressable memory. The paper repeatedly contrasts this with “local” attention and claims that the model can consider the entire video while generating each word, but conventional soft attention over all encoder states already has this property. The added recurrent memory may still be useful, but the conceptual distinction should be sharpened.

Relatedly, the novelty relative to existing attention and reviewer-style captioning models is not fully convincing. Conditioning attention on prior decoder state and previous contextual information is common in sequence-to-sequence attention models. The paper argues that prior work does not explicitly store all previous attention, but the actual implementation does not seem to store the full sequence of past attention maps either. It instead summarizes prior decisions through an LSTM state. This may be a reasonable design, but the paper should be more careful in claiming a qualitatively different memory architecture. A stronger discussion would explain whether the benefit comes from adding another recurrent layer between attention and decoding, from using convolutional feature maps with spatial attention, from conditioning attention on a memory state, or from some combination of these.

The experimental evidence does not adequately isolate these factors. The ablation table is useful, but the results are somewhat confusing and do not strongly support all claims. On MSVD, the full HAM+TEM model obtains the best METEOR among the ablations, but HAM without TEM has better BLEU-2, BLEU-3, BLEU-4, and similar CIDEr, while the simpler attention model has better CIDEr than the full model. Thus, the conclusion that TEM and HAM complement each other is only partially supported. If the full model is truly better, this should be shown consistently across metrics or explained in terms of metric tradeoffs. It would also be important to report variance over multiple runs, because the absolute differences are small and could plausibly be due to random initialization, frame sampling, or hyperparameter search.

There are also inconsistencies in the experimental setup that make interpretation difficult. The ablation study uses 40 frames per video, while the main MSVD comparison uses 8 frames per video. This makes it hard to understand how frame sampling affects the architecture, especially since the temporal encoder is one of the proposed contributions. If the temporal modeling component is important, one would expect a clear analysis of performance as a function of the number of sampled frames. The paper should also clarify whether hyperparameters were tuned separately for each ablation and baseline, or whether the full model received more tuning. Since the reported hidden sizes are quite large and selected by random search, fairness of comparison matters.

The state-of-the-art claim is overstated. On MSVD, the model has strong BLEU scores among methods that do not use external motion/action features, but it is not best on all metrics. Another method in the table has substantially higher METEOR, and methods using additional features outperform the proposed model on BLEU-4 and METEOR. It is fair to say that the method is competitive and achieves the best reported BLEU-4 among the listed methods without external features, but the abstract and conclusion claim more broadly that it outperforms all previously proposed methods and reaches a new state of the art. The tables do not support that broad statement. The Charades results show an improvement over the dataset baseline, but the gain is modest, and the absolute scores remain far below human performance. More analysis is needed before drawing strong conclusions about action understanding.

Another concern is that the paper does not provide enough implementation detail for reproducibility. The frame sampling strategy is not fully specified. The decoding procedure is also missing important details: whether greedy decoding or beam search is used, what beam size is selected, how unknown words are handled, and how maximum caption length is chosen. The preprocessing description says NLTK tokenization is used, but does not describe vocabulary thresholds, handling of rare words, start/end tokens, or whether captions are lowercased. The architecture section gives equations for the main modules, but some dimensions and definitions are unclear, especially in the attention update where repeated decoder and memory states are concatenated or broadcast across frames. The loss function omits the decoder parameters from the listed parameter set. These issues are not necessarily fatal, but they reduce confidence in the empirical results.

The qualitative examples reveal another limitation: the model can generate fluent but semantically incorrect captions, sometimes hallucinating common objects or actions. This is a known issue in captioning systems, but it is particularly relevant here because the paper claims that the memory-augmented attention improves reasoning over actions and temporal structure. Some of the incorrect examples suggest that the model may rely heavily on language priors. A more convincing evaluation would include attention visualizations over time and space, showing whether the model attends to action-relevant frames when generating verbs and to object-relevant regions when generating nouns. Since the core contribution is an attention/memory mechanism, visualizing or quantitatively evaluating its behavior would be very valuable.

I have several questions for the authors. In what precise sense does the model store all previous attention decisions, rather than only a recurrent summary of them? Have you compared HAM to a standard attention decoder with an additional recurrent layer of comparable parameter count, to rule out the possibility that the gains come from increased capacity? How sensitive are the results to the number of sampled frames, and why are 40 frames used in the ablation while 8 or 16 frames are used in the main experiments? Were the ablation models given the same hyperparameter search budget as the full model? What decoding method and beam size were used for the reported results? Can the authors provide attention visualizations or an analysis showing that the memory state reduces repeated attention or improves verb generation? Finally, can the claims of state-of-the-art performance be revised to reflect the mixed results across metrics and feature settings?

Overall, I find the direction promising and the architecture reasonable, but the submission currently falls short of the level of clarity and empirical rigor I would expect for acceptance. The idea of conditioning video attention on a recurrent memory of prior attended context is worthwhile, and the results are competitive. Nevertheless, the novelty over standard recurrent attention mechanisms is not sharply established, the ablations do not clearly validate the claimed contributions, and some claims are stronger than the evidence supports. My recommendation is rejection in its current form, with encouragement to resubmit after clarifying the memory mechanism, strengthening the baselines and ablations, reporting more reproducible experimental details, and tempering the performance claims.

The paper studies the expressive power of deep neural networks through the behavior of random networks and then uses the resulting picture to motivate several observations about trained networks. The central object is the length of a one-dimensional input trajectory after it is propagated through successive layers. The authors argue, both theoretically and empirically, that this trajectory length grows exponentially with depth for random hard-tanh networks in appropriate parameter regimes. They then connect this growth to three proposed measures of expressivity: the number of neuron transitions along an input curve, the number of activation patterns, and the number of dichotomies realized when sweeping weights along a trajectory. A further consequence is that earlier layers should have more influence on expressivity because they have more remaining depth above them; this is tested by freezing all but one layer in networks trained on MNIST and CIFAR-10. Finally, the authors examine how training changes trajectory length, suggesting that training may trade off expressivity and stability depending on initialization scale.

The paper is ambitious and addresses an important conceptual issue: why depth appears to provide representational advantages beyond merely increasing parameter count. I found the trajectory-length perspective appealing. It gives an intuitive geometric mechanism by which depth can amplify complexity: a simple input curve can be stretched and folded repeatedly through the network, creating more crossings of decision boundaries and more activation changes. This is a useful complement to existing work based on worst-case constructions or counting linear regions. In particular, focusing on random networks is a valuable choice, since random initialization is the starting point for modern training procedures and may be more representative than hand-designed weights used in some expressivity separations.

A major strength of the paper is that it tries to unify several notions of expressivity under a single geometric quantity. The connection between trajectory length and transitions is intuitive and well supported by the figures.
